mnemonics.title: Mean Variance Formula
mnemonics.content: Remember: VAR = E[X²] - (E[X])² → Think 'VEVEE': Variance Equals Variance of Expectation squared. Or simply: Var = Mean of squares - Square of mean.

mnemonics.title: Binomial Mean and Variance
mnemonics.content: Mean = n×p (n times 'p' for probability of success). Variance = n×p×(1-p) = n×p×q where q = (1-p). Think: 'n-p-q'.

mnemonics.title: Law of Total Variance
mnemonics.content: Remember as 'TAN': Total = Average + Variance. Total variance = Expected conditional variance + Variance of conditional expectation.

mnemonics.title: Standard Deviation
mnemonics.content: SD = √Variance. Think: SD 'stands' for the square root of variance, taking it back to original units.

acronyms.usage: Random variables that are independent and have the same probability distribution. Example: Multiple coin flips.
For continuous random variables, describes the probability of taking on a given value.
Gives P(X ≤ x), the probability that X is at most x.
For discrete random variables, gives P(X = x) for each possible value.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a target: tight clustering around bullseye = low variance; scattered shots = high variance. The average squared distance from the center represents variance.
Think of expectation like a lever - it balances at the mean. Adding constants shifts the lever; scaling multiplies distances from center.
Covariance as the 'slope' of relationship: positive = both increase together (↗↗), negative = one increases while other decreases (↗↘).

analogies.analogy: Like the center of gravity or balance point of a distribution - where a seesaw would balance if weights were placed at each value.
Think of variance as 'messiness' in data. Low variance = neat, organized data around a single point; high variance = messy, scattered data.
Average 'surprise' or distance from the mean. If on average you're 5 units away from the mean, that's your standard deviation.
Two people walking together: positive covariance = they move in same direction; negative = opposite directions; zero = independent walkers not affecting each other.
